role_hints.note: Rainmaking, courtroom advocacy, negotiation, signing pleadings and holding the license/liability — AI assists but cannot legally perform these.
Research, memo drafting, doc review and first-draft contracts are heavily AI-exposed; appearances, depositions, client counseling are not.
Document assembly, discovery review, e-filing and cite-checking are directly automatable; witness/client coordination less so.
Scheduling, dictation, formatting, intake paperwork — near-fully automatable.
Time-entry, prebills and collections automate; vendor and HR judgment persists.
Mixed bag; records/docketing automatable, business development is relationship work.

labor_roles: BLS OEWS industry table, NAICS 5411 Legal Services (use 541110 detail where published) — annual May release
ABA Profile of the Legal Profession — American Bar Association, annual
NALP (National Association for Law Placement) associate salary surveys, annual
capture: Clio Legal Trends Report — Clio, annual
Thomson Reuters State of the Legal Market (with Georgetown Law) — annual
The Justice Gap Report — Legal Services Corporation, periodic (latent consumer demand)
adoption: ABA Legal Technology Survey Report — ABA Legal Technology Resource Center, annual
Thomson Reuters Future of Professionals Report — annual
Wolters Kluwer Future Ready Lawyer Survey — annual
firm_counts: Census Bureau SUSB / County Business Patterns, NAICS 541110
ABA National Lawyer Population Survey — annual
Economic Census (NAICS 541110 receipts by legal specialty), quinquennial
multiples: BVR DealStats — Business Valuation Resources (sparse arms-length law firm comps)
BizBuySell Insight Report — quarterly (small consumer practices)
Trade coverage of MSO/ABS deals (e.g., Above the Law / ALM reporting) — note true comps scarce due to Rule 5.4

capture_questions: Is the client relationship owned by the individual partner (portable book of business) or the firm brand — what share of revenue survives a rainmaker's exit?
For consumer/SMB matters (wills, LLCs, uncontested divorce, demand letters), how much volume can bypass lawyers entirely via generic AI plus e-filing before UPL enforcement matters?
Are corporate clients pulling routine work in-house (GC plus AI), leaving firms only litigation appearances and bet-the-company judgment?
What fraction of billings rests on procedural mandates (court appearances, signed filings, notarized/attorney-certified documents) that AI cannot legally perform?
541110.terminal_value_question: Five to ten years out, does paid demand persist because licensure, rights of audience, and malpractice liability require a human signature — or does the billable-hour base collapse to a thin stamp-and-judgment layer while AI does the underlying work, with latent demand (justice gap) volume only partially offsetting price deflation?
541110.special_notes: Non-lawyer ownership is banned under state versions of ABA Model Rule 5.4 everywhere except Arizona ABS licenses and Utah's regulatory sandbox — PE elsewhere must use MSO (management services organization) structures whose enforceability and economics the scorer should verify; severely limits buyout feasibility (B). Hourly-billing deflation risk is direct: AI compresses billable hours unless firms reprice to flat/value fees. High key-person risk — client books are partner-portable. Segment economics diverge sharply: plaintiff contingency (litigation-finance adjacent), insurance defense (hourly, rate-capped), consumer flat-fee. Large cohort of retiring solo/small-firm owners creates succession-driven supply of sellers.

role_hints.role: Owner notaries / principal signing agents (incl. draws)
role_hints.note: The commissioned human witnessing act is the statutory product; AI/RON platforms automate everything around it but a human must still notarize in every state.
Loan-signing appointments require physical or live-video presence; scheduling, document prep and journaling are automatable.
Order intake, appointment routing and confirmation calls are fully automatable — platforms already do this.
Physical document transport persists only where wet-ink signatures are still required; e-closing adoption erodes it.

labor_roles: BLS OEWS, NAICS 5411 (541120 rarely broken out — use 5412/5411 context plus Census receipts)
National Notary Association (NNA) member and fee surveys, periodic
capture: ALTA Digital Closing Survey — American Land Title Association, annual
Snapdocs eClosing/signing volume reports (vendor-published)
MBA Mortgage Finance Forecast — Mortgage Bankers Association, monthly (derivative demand)
adoption: NNA remote online notarization (RON) state-enactment tracking
Proof (formerly Notarize) usage disclosures (vendor)
MISMO RON standards adoption updates
firm_counts: Census Bureau SUSB / CBP, NAICS 541120 (very small establishment counts)
State commissioning counts as compiled by the NNA
multiples: BizBuySell Insight Report (rare listings)
BVR DealStats — expect near-empty comp set; treat absence of a market as a finding

capture_questions: Do title companies and lenders route signings through platforms (Snapdocs, Proof, DocuSign Notary), making the individual notary interchangeable gig labor with no client ownership?
As RON scales, do platform network effects capture the margin, leaving commissioned notaries as per-act piece labor at statutory capped fees?
What share of demand is statutory witnessing that survives if states or federal law accept cryptographic digital-ID verification without a human witness?
541120.terminal_value_question: The service exists purely by statute: if digital identity verification is ever accepted without a commissioned human witness, paid notarization disappears; if the human requirement persists, the sharpest question is whether RON platforms rather than notary offices capture the remaining economics.
541120.special_notes: Most establishments are solo/mobile operators with near-zero transferable enterprise value — distinguish 'industry exists' from 'acquirable firms exist.' Per-act fees are capped by statute in many states (built-in price ceiling). Demand is derivative of mortgage/real-estate transaction volume (highly cyclical). RON is now authorized in the large majority of states — the platform layer is the capture point, not the notary. Do not conflate with 541191 title/settlement, where notarization is one embedded step.

role_hints.note: Underwriter appointments, licensure and realtor/lender relationships; AI does not replace the relationship or the license.
File coordination, CD/settlement-statement prep and disbursement checks automate; licensed escrow duties and closing-table work remain.
Automated search on digitized county records is advanced; old/unindexed records, legal descriptions and curative judgment still need humans.
Order entry, payoff requests, recording and policy issuance are workflow tasks AI handles well.
Order flow is won through personal relationships with realtors, lenders and builders — the actual moat.
Reception, scheduling, document handling.

labor_roles: BLS OEWS, NAICS 5411 Legal Services with 541191 detail where published — annual
ALTA compensation and operations benchmarking surveys — American Land Title Association
capture: ALTA Title Insurance Market Share Data (from statutory filings) — quarterly
The Title Report — October Research, ongoing (industry structure and M&A)
FHFA/Fannie Mae title acceptance and attorney-opinion-letter pilot announcements
adoption: ALTA Digital Closing Survey — annual
MISMO eMortgage/RON adoption statistics
firm_counts: State insurance department title agent licensee rolls
multiples: The Title Report M&A coverage — October Research
FNF and First American investor disclosures on agency acquisitions
BVR DealStats (small agency comps)

capture_questions: Who originates the order — realtor, lender, builder or attorney — and does the firm own any repeat relationship, or is flow captive to affiliated business arrangements (JVs with brokerages)?
Will GSE title-waiver and attorney-opinion-letter programs let lenders bypass full title search/settlement on low-risk refinances, and how much of volume is that?
Are the four national underwriters (FNF, First American, Old Republic, Stewart) vertically integrating direct operations and squeezing independent agents' premium splits?
541191.terminal_value_question: If AI title search on digitized records plus GSE acceptance of alternative title assurance matures, does the paid abstract/settlement function shrink to insurance distribution plus curative work on hard titles — or do regulated premium structures and upstream referral capture preserve revenue regardless of the labor content?
541191.special_notes: Order flow is controlled upstream by lenders and realtors — the firm often does not own its demand (key C-factor trap). Affiliated business arrangements and RESPA constraints shape who can be paid for referrals. Title insurance rates are state-regulated (promulgated in TX, filed elsewhere) — regulation can either protect margin or force deflation. Attorney-closing states (e.g., GA, SC, MA) shift settlement work into 541110. Extreme mortgage-cycle sensitivity — normalize any revenue year against MBA origination volume. Underwriter appointment and state escrow/title licensure are the entry barriers, not skill.

role_hints.role: Owner-operators (doc preparers, process serving firm owners, patent agents; incl. draws)
role_hints.note: Mix of relationship management and hands-on production; production half is AI-exposed.
Physical service of process cannot be done by AI — but can be abolished by e-service court rules, a regulatory not technological risk.
Form completion and filing packets are precisely what generic consumer AI does; most exposed role in legal services.
AI drafts specifications/claims; USPTO registration and prosecution signature stay human.
Job routing, affidavit prep and status tracking are software tasks already.

labor_roles: BLS OEWS, NAICS 5411 (541199 detail where published)
NALA Utilization and Compensation Survey — paralegal association, periodic
NAPPS (National Association of Professional Process Servers) member data
capture: LegalZoom 10-K / investor filings (consumer doc-prep economics)
National Center for State Courts (NCSC) e-filing and e-service rule tracking
USPTO registered practitioner roster (patent agent counts)
adoption: NCSC state court technology surveys
Process-serving software vendor industry surveys (e.g., ServeManager/Lawgical)
LegalZoom and Rocket Lawyer product disclosures on AI features
multiples: BizBuySell Insight Report
BVR DealStats (sparse; mostly process-serving and doc-prep micro-deals)

capture_questions: Can consumers and small businesses go straight to generic AI for the forms and filings that document preparers sell, given there is no licensure requirement protecting the niche?
For process serving, who owns the relationship — law firms and collections agencies dispatch the work — and do court e-service rule changes eliminate the product by statute?
Do UPL boundaries (which both cap what non-lawyers may sell and create their niche) tighten or loosen as AI legal tools spread?
541199.terminal_value_question: Legal document preparation is among the most directly AI-substitutable paid services anywhere; the sharp question is whether anything in this code besides physical service of process and USPTO-registered patent prosecution retains a reason to be paid for once consumer AI matures.
541199.special_notes: Catch-all code — top-3 revenue sub-segments: (1) legal document preparation services (likely dominant by revenue, includes online platform operations), (2) process serving (largest by establishment count, fragmented, physical), (3) patent agents and independent/freelance paralegal services. Score them separately — their AI exposure and moats differ completely. E-service/e-filing rule changes are an existential regulatory risk to process serving independent of AI. Very small average firm size; verify whether acquirable EBITDA exists at all outside a few regional process-serving consolidators.

role_hints.note: Audit opinions and return sign-off are licensure-gated; client relationships and advisory judgment are the durable human layer.
Audit fieldwork, workpapers, tie-outs and tax prep are heavily AI-exposed; professional-skepticism judgment calls less so.
Write-up, reconciliation and close work is near-fully automatable by platform AI.
Form-driven prep automates; review and signature migrate up to CPAs.
Scheduling, assembly, e-file transmittal, engagement letters.
Growing CAS/advisory arm; AI-augmented rather than replaced near-term.

labor_roles: BLS OEWS, NAICS 5412 Accounting/Tax/Bookkeeping/Payroll with 541211 detail where published — annual
The Rosenberg National Survey of CPA Firm Statistics — annual
AICPA PCPS/CPA.com National MAP Survey — biennial
Inside Public Accounting (IPA) Benchmarking Report — annual
capture: Thomson Reuters State of Tax Professionals Report — annual
Intuit 10-K (QuickBooks Live / TurboTax assisted encroachment)
AICPA Trends Report (pipeline/supply of CPAs) — periodic
adoption: Wolters Kluwer Annual Accounting Industry Survey
CPA.com / AICPA technology adoption surveys
Thomson Reuters Future of Professionals Report — annual
firm_counts: NASBA / state boards of accountancy firm registrations
Accounting Today Top 100 Firms — annual (upper tier)
multiples: Accounting Practice Sales published market data (revenue-multiple benchmarks for small practices)
Poe Group Advisors practice-sale data
Koltin Consulting Group commentary on PE deal terms
The Rosenberg Survey valuation sections

capture_questions: Is audit/attest demand payer-mandated (lenders, regulators, bonding, peer review) such that clients cannot bypass the firm no matter how good AI gets?
For tax and client accounting services, do Intuit, IRS Direct File and generic AI let SMB clients self-serve, or do complexity and liability keep them paying?
How much revenue is partner-relationship advisory versus commodity compliance, and does the relationship survive partner retirement (succession wave)?
541211.terminal_value_question: Attestation is a legally required signature — the question is whether AI collapses realization on the ~80% of hours that are fieldwork and prep faster than shortage-driven price increases offset it, i.e., whether audit and complex tax become a smaller-headcount stamp-and-judgment business with preserved or even improved economics.
541211.special_notes: Alternative practice structures make PE ownership feasible (attest entity stays CPA-owned; non-attest ServiceCo is investor-owned) — an active roll-up wave has run since ~2021 (EisnerAmper, Citrin Cooperman and many followers); scorer should verify current deal pace and structures. State board rules and peer review still apply. The 150-hour rule and staffing pipeline shortage currently support pricing — a countervailing force against AI deflation. Multiples trap: small-practice comps quote multiples of revenue with owner comp inside; normalize partner compensation before comparing to EBITDA multiples on PE deals. Independence rules restrict cross-selling to attest clients.

role_hints.note: Interview-driven 1040 prep on top of software is the canonical AI-automatable task; already largely software-guided.
Local marketing, hiring and multi-unit operations; the client file often belongs to the brand/franchise system.
Quality review and exception handling; AI review tools compress this layer.
Appointment setting, document intake and status calls are fully automatable.

labor_roles: BLS OEWS, NAICS 541213 detail where published (else 5412) — annual
NATP Tax Professional Fee Study — National Association of Tax Professionals, periodic
NSA Income and Fees Survey — National Society of Accountants, biennial
capture: IRS e-file and Direct File usage statistics — annual filing-season data
H&R Block 10-K and investor materials (assisted vs DIY client counts)
Intuit 10-K (TurboTax, full-service vs DIY mix)
adoption: IRS Return Preparer Office PTIN statistics
H&R Block and Intuit investor-day disclosures on AI-assisted prep
firm_counts: IRS PTIN holder counts by preparer type
Franchise Disclosure Documents (H&R Block, Liberty Tax, Jackson Hewitt) for unit counts
multiples: Accounting Practice Sales (tax practice listings and multiples)
Franchise resale data from FDDs and franchise brokers
BizBuySell Insight Report

capture_questions: What share of clients are EITC/refund-advance filers who come for the money product, not the prep — and does that embedded-lending relationship insulate the storefront from free AI filing?
Does IRS Direct File plus generic consumer AI eliminate the paid simple-1040 segment, and how fast is state-level adoption of direct filing expanding?
In franchise systems, who owns the client file — brand or franchisee — and what does that do to a local acquisition's durability?
541213.terminal_value_question: Simple-return preparation is arguably the most AI-terminal paid service in this whole set: within 5-10 years, does storefront prep survive only as embedded refund lending (transfers/advances) plus complex, small-business, immigrant and non-filer-resolution niches — and what fraction of today's revenue do those represent?
541213.special_notes: No federal licensure — a PTIN suffices; only a few states (CA via CTEC, OR, MD, NY) regulate preparers, so barriers are minimal and the moat is brand plus foot traffic. Extreme seasonality (most revenue Jan–Apr) distorts labor and margin comparisons. Reported revenue often bundles financial products (refund transfers, advances) — separate them when scoring deflation. Do not blend with 541211: complex/business returns behave like CPA work; this code is dominated by consumer 1040 volume. Franchise royalty loads change unit economics versus independents.

role_hints.note: Data entry, exception handling and off-cycle runs are core AI/RPA territory; already shrinking per client.
Tier-1 inquiries automate via AI chat; escalations involving money movement and tax notices keep humans.
Distribution and accountant-channel relationships are the industry's real competitive weapon.
Data migration and setup are increasingly tool-assisted; complex multi-state onboarding persists.
Concentrated at platform players; AI-augmented rather than eliminated.

role_hints.role: Management / owners (incl. draws at independent bureaus)
role_hints.note: Compliance oversight and client relationships at local bureaus.

labor_roles: BLS OEWS, NAICS 541214 detail where published (else 5412) — annual
PayrollOrg (formerly American Payroll Association) compensation and operations surveys
capture: ADP 10-K and investor materials (client counts, retention, client-funds float)
Paychex 10-K and investor materials
NelsonHall Payroll Services NEAT assessments — periodic
adoption: PayrollOrg 'Getting Paid In America' survey — annual
Everest Group Multi-Country Payroll PEAK Matrix — periodic
NelsonHall payroll BPO market analyses
firm_counts: ADP/Paychex competitive-landscape disclosures (independent bureau counts referenced in filings)
multiples: Software Equity Group vertical software / HCM M&A reports
Houlihan Lokey HCM market updates — periodic
BVR DealStats (local bureau comps)

capture_questions: Can independent bureaus survive ADP/Paychex scale plus embedded payroll (Gusto, Square, banks, vertical SaaS) that owns the SMB relationship at the point of banking or POS?
Does the accountant-referral channel — which often controls the SMB decision — migrate to platform partner programs, stranding local bureaus?
Could AI agents filing directly with IRS/state systems disintermediate processors, or do tax-remittance liability, float economics and money-movement trust preserve the middleman?
541214.terminal_value_question: Payroll is already software, so the terminal risk is not AI doing the calculation but AI agents plus government e-file APIs letting employers bypass processors entirely; the counterweight is that remittance liability, client-funds float and multi-state compliance complexity have kept this an entrenched scale business for decades.
541214.special_notes: Scale oligopoly: ADP and Paychex dominate, earning float income on client funds that independents cannot match — gatekeeper/oligopoly capture is the central structural fact. The acquirable pool is independent local bureaus, which face simultaneous squeeze from national platforms and embedded fintech payroll. Do not confuse with PEOs (NAICS 561330) or staffing. Money-transmission, IRS reporting-agent and NACHA rules are real barriers but also fixed-cost burdens on small targets. Per-employee-per-month pricing faces deflation from embedded competitors selling payroll at near-zero margin as a wedge.

role_hints.note: Bank-feed categorization, reconciliation and monthly close are already being automated natively inside QuickBooks/Xero.

role_hints.role: Owner-operators (non-CPA accountants, enrolled agents; incl. draws)
role_hints.note: Client relationships, cleanup judgment and fractional-controller advice; production share of their time is AI-exposed.
Invoice generation, AR follow-up and statement processing are workflow automation targets.
Scheduling, document chasing, client onboarding paperwork.
Adjacent compliance tasks; overlaps 541213/541214 offerings at small firms.

labor_roles: BLS OEWS, NAICS 541219 detail where published (else 5412) — annual
NSA Income and Fees Survey — biennial (covers non-CPA practitioners)
NACPB (National Association of Certified Public Bookkeepers) surveys
capture: Intuit 10-K and investor materials (QuickBooks Live and AI-native bookkeeping expansion)
Xero small business and accountant-ecosystem reports
Intuit ProAdvisor ecosystem disclosures
adoption: Accounting Today 'Year Ahead' technology survey — Arizent, annual
Intuit/Xero accountant-channel technology surveys
multiples: Accounting Practice Sales (bookkeeping practice multiples)
Poe Group Advisors
BizBuySell Insight Report

capture_questions: Intuit owns the ledger: does QuickBooks Live plus native AI categorization convert bookkeeping clients away from independent firms, or turn firms into channel partners on Intuit's terms?
Is the client relationship personal to the owner-bookkeeper, and does it survive an acquisition and owner exit?
How exposed is billing-services revenue to clients' own ERP/AI automation eliminating the outsourced function?
541219.terminal_value_question: Transaction categorization, reconciliation and close are among the nearest-term fully automatable white-collar tasks; does any paid human demand remain in 5-10 years beyond cleanup, oversight and fractional-controller advisory once bank-feed AI matures inside the platforms themselves?
541219.special_notes: Catch-all code — top-3 revenue sub-segments: (1) bookkeeping/write-up services (dominant), (2) billing and invoicing services, (3) other non-CPA accounting work (enrolled agents' accounting practices, outsourced controller/CFO services). No licensure moat at all — anyone may bookkeep — so entry is free and offshore substitution has already deflated pricing; easy to buy but weak capture and terminal value must be scored on evidence, not assumed. Platform capture by Intuit/Xero is the central structural question. Tiny average firm size with succession-driven sellers — verify transferable EBITDA exists.

role_hints.note: Stamp, code responsibility, client trust and design judgment stay human; their production hours (drawings, specs) are AI-exposed.
Construction-document production — the largest fee phase — is the most directly automatable work in the firm.
Consultant coordination and CA (construction administration) site work persist; scheduling/RFI paperwork automates.
Generative layout and FF&E documentation automate; client taste-making does not.
Proposals, qualifications packages and admin are heavily automatable.
Renderings and spec assembly are already AI-commoditized.

labor_roles: BLS OEWS, NAICS 5413 with 541310 detail where published — annual
AIA Firm Survey Report — American Institute of Architects, periodic (~every 2-3 years)
AIA Compensation & Benefits Report — periodic
capture: AIA Architecture Billings Index (ABI) — monthly (demand cycle)
AIA Firm Survey (client and sector mix)
DBIA/FMI design-build market studies — periodic (contractor capture of design)
adoption: Deltek Clarity Architecture & Engineering Industry Study — annual (has AI adoption chapters)
AIA technology and AI reports
firm_counts: NCARB by the Numbers — annual (licensee counts)
multiples: Zweig Group Valuation Report / M&A surveys — annual
Morrissey Goodale AE M&A deal data — ongoing
Rusk O'Brien Gido + Partners A/E valuation studies

capture_questions: Do owners/developers hire the architect for design judgment and brand, or only because permits require a stamped set — and how much of the fee sits in automatable production phases versus relationship phases?
Does design-build growth shift the client relationship to contractors, reducing architects to subcontracted stamp providers?
In residential and light-commercial work (often exempt from architect requirements), does AI design-to-permit tooling bypass architects entirely?
541310.terminal_value_question: If AI can generate code-compliant permit sets, architecture bifurcates into a liability/stamp utility plus a taste-and-brand layer at the high end — the sharp question is whether permitting authorities and insurers keep requiring a licensed human in responsible charge, preserving the toll even as production fees deflate.
541310.special_notes: Licensure stamp is the moat but invites 'plan-stamping' commoditization at the bottom. Several states restrict architecture firm ownership to licensed professionals (New York is notably strict; rules vary widely) — check the state mix of any target before assuming PE acquisition is clean; A&E consolidators are increasingly buying architecture bolt-ons regardless. Fees are concentrated in construction-document production, the phase most exposed to AI deflation. ABI-tracked cyclicality is severe. Residential and interiors segments are partially outside licensure requirements — different exposure profile within the same code.

role_hints.role: Licensed landscape architects incl. principals (incl. draws)
role_hints.note: Stamped grading/stormwater/public-realm documents and client relationships stay human; planting plans and renderings are AI-exposed.
Site-plan production, planting schedules and renders are directly automatable.
Agency coordination and construction observation persist; documentation automates.
Proposals and RFP responses dominate; highly automatable.
Physical site presence for observation and approvals.

labor_roles: BLS OEWS, NAICS 5413 with 541320 detail where published — annual
ASLA (American Society of Landscape Architects) compensation and business surveys — periodic
CLARB licensure data
capture: ASLA business/practice surveys (client and prime-vs-sub mix)
NALP (National Association of Landscape Professionals — the contractors) design-build market data
Census construction put-in-place series (derivative demand)
adoption: Deltek Clarity A&E Industry Study — annual
ASLA technology surveys
multiples: Zweig Group Valuation Report — annual
Morrissey Goodale AE M&A data
BizBuySell (small design studio deals)

capture_questions: Is the firm prime to the owner/municipality, or a sub-consultant to architects/engineers/developers who own the relationship and can swap or absorb the LA scope?
Does residential and commercial design-build by landscape contractors bypass landscape architects except where stamped grading/stormwater documents are required?
What share of fees is code-required work (stormwater, ADA, public projects) versus discretionary amenity design that clients can generate with AI tools?
541320.terminal_value_question: Discretionary planting and amenity design is highly AI-exposed and, in title-act states, not even legally reserved to licensees; durable paid demand concentrates in stamped site-civil-adjacent and public work — the question is how much of the current fee pool that regulated core actually represents.
541320.special_notes: Licensure exists nearly everywhere but several states have title acts only (the work itself can be done unlicensed — materially weaker moat than 541310/541330; verify practice-act vs title-act by state). Firms are very small with thin transferable EBITDA. Frequently sub-consultant economics — the C factor should be scored on who holds the prime contract. Acronym trap: NALP means both the landscape contractors' association and the law-placement association. Demand rides construction and municipal spending cycles.

role_hints.role: Licensed professional engineers incl. principals (incl. draws)
role_hints.note: Engineer-of-record stamp, liability and client trust are non-automatable; their analysis and report-writing hours are heavily AI-exposed.
Calculations, modeling and report drafting automate substantially; field verification and judgment calls persist.
Lab/materials testing and data collection partly physical; data reduction automates.
Production drawings are the most automatable cost line in the firm.
Client/agency coordination persists; schedule, budget and submittal paperwork automates.
Physical site presence (construction inspection, surveying) — AI-resistant, drone-augmented.
Proposal production for QBS pursuits is a large, automatable overhead.

labor_roles: BLS OEWS, NAICS 5413 with 541330 detail where published — annual
Zweig Group salary surveys — annual
Deltek Clarity A&E Industry Study — annual
capture: ACEC Engineering Business Sentiment survey — ACEC Research Institute, quarterly
Deltek Clarity (client mix, backlog)
ENR Top 500 Design Firms — Engineering News-Record, annual (market structure)
adoption: Deltek Clarity A&E Study AI chapters — annual
ACEC Research Institute technology/AI reports
firm_counts: ENR rankings for the upper tier
multiples: Morrissey Goodale AE M&A deal data and commentary — ongoing
Zweig Group Valuation Report — annual
EFCG (Environmental Financial Consulting Group) benchmarking and M&A data

capture_questions: Public clients (DOTs, municipalities) select via QBS with audited overhead rates — do AI productivity gains get clawed back through FAR overhead audits and fee schedules rather than retained as firm margin?
Is the engineer-of-record relationship institutional (on-call contracts, framework agreements held by the firm) or personal to individual principals?
Can industrial and developer clients insource design with AI plus a single stamping PE, and does that 'plan-stamp' pattern spread despite responsible-charge rules?
541330.terminal_value_question: Stamps, EOR liability and infrastructure-funding demand make paid demand robust near-term; the 5-10 year question is whether AI-collapsed production hours turn engineering into a smaller-headcount stamp-and-liability business — and whether public fee structures (multiplier on audited cost) hand the productivity gain to clients instead of owners.
541330.special_notes: Active, well-documented PE consolidation wave — dozens of platforms rolling up civil, MEP, structural and environmental firms; verify current pace via Morrissey Goodale deal counts. Licensure: every state requires a PE in responsible charge and firm registration; a minority impose professional-ownership requirements on engineering entities — check target-state rules, but the deal wave shows structures exist. Cost-plus/audited-overhead public contracts can convert AI efficiency into client savings, not margin — a subtle deflation channel the scorer must model. Field, survey and inspection revenue is physical and AI-resistant. Sub-segment mix (transportation, water, environmental, MEP, geotech) drives both cyclicality and AI exposure.

role_hints.note: 2D/3D production drafting is the product itself and the single most directly AI-automatable service in this whole industry set.
Client account management with A/E firms and manufacturers; production oversight shrinks with automation.
Drawing QA increasingly automated; liability-driven human review persists at the margin.
Physical site presence for scan-to-BIM and as-built measurement — the defensible niche.
Job intake, file transmittal, invoicing.

labor_roles: BLS OEWS, NAICS 5413 (541340 detail where published) — annual
ADDA (American Design Drafting Association) member data — sparse; treat thinness as a finding
capture: Deltek Clarity A&E Study (A/E outsourcing behavior)
Autodesk investor materials and product disclosures (generative CAD/AI roadmap)
adoption: Autodesk and CAD-vendor AI feature adoption disclosures
Deltek Clarity technology chapters — annual
firm_counts: Census Bureau SUSB / CBP, NAICS 541340 (small establishment counts)
multiples: BizBuySell Insight Report
BVR DealStats — expect very sparse comps; absence of an M&A market is itself the data point

capture_questions: Clients are A/E firms and manufacturers outsourcing overflow production — as those clients adopt generative CAD internally, does third-party drafting demand evaporate before anything else in this set?
Is any client relationship durable beyond price-per-sheet, given offshore drafting bureaus already commoditized the work?
Does scan-to-BIM / as-built field capture (requiring physical presence) become the only defensible revenue line, and how big is it within current revenue?
541340.terminal_value_question: Drafting is the most directly AI-terminal code here: mature text/model-to-CAD plausibly eliminates paid third-party production drafting within the 5-10 year window, leaving only reality capture (site scanning) and legacy-drawing conversion — quantify what share of today's revenue those niches represent.
541340.special_notes: No licensure moat of any kind — drafters work under clients' professionals' stamps, so there is no regulatory toll to capture. Pricing already deflated by offshore bureaus; AI is a second, steeper deflation on top. Double exposure: demand is derivative of A/E and manufacturing clients who are themselves automating, so the outsourced layer disappears first. Establishments are tiny with minimal transferable EBITDA — verify any acquirable firms exist beyond scan-to-BIM specialists. Rough revenue split to check: architectural/construction drafting (dominant), mechanical/manufacturing drafting, civil/survey drafting support.
